title: Overfitting a Single Batch
concept: Overfitting a single batch of data is a diagnostic test. If a CNN cannot achieve 100% accuracy on a tiny batch of 4 images, it has a core bug in its architecture, loss, or optimization code.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What should you do if both training and validation loss remain high and flat?

options.text: Increase model capacity (more layers/filters) or train for more epochs, as the model is underfitting
Add aggressive dropout and data augmentation to make training harder
Stop training immediately because the model has converged

exercises.explain: Yes! High, flat loss indicates underfitting, which requires more capacity or time.

cards.title: Finding Hidden Bugs
cards.body: When a network fails to learn, it is hard to isolate the cause. Is it the dataset? The optimizer? A broken gradient update? To find out, we run a diagnostic test: we shrink our entire dataset down to just **four images** and check if the model can memorize them perfectly.

cards.title: Overfitting a Single Batch
cards.body: Overfitting a single batch is a sanity check where you train your model on a tiny subset of data (e.g. 2-8 images) with **zero regularization** (no augmentation, no dropout). If the network is coded correctly, it should easily achieve 100% accuracy (near-zero loss) within a few epochs.

cards.title: The open-book pop quiz
cards.body: If a student is failing open-book quizzes where the questions and answers are right in front of them (overfitting 4 images), they probably can't read the language or have a broken pencil. If your model can't memorize 4 images, it has a fundamental structural flaw.

cards.title: The broken sign change
cards.body: If you accidentally put a plus instead of a minus in your loss gradient calculation, weights will move in the wrong direction. By trying to overfit 4 images, you will notice immediately: the loss will climb or stay flat. This isolates the bug to code logic.

cards.title: It's not a waste of time
cards.body: It feels counterintuitive to intentionally overfit your model. We are taught that overfitting is bad! But in this specific diagnostic test, **failure to overfit** is a critical clue. If the model can't learn a tiny batch, it is mathematically incapable of learning a large dataset.

cards.title: Batch Overfitting Results
cards.body: What the single-batch diagnostic check reveals:

left.text:
Model code is mathematically correct
Optimizer is updating weights
Problem is likely dataset size/quality

right.text:
Core bug in model code
Check loss function, gradients, or learning rate
Do not train on full data yet

exercises.prompt: What does a model failing to overfit a single batch of 4 images indicate?

options.text: A fundamental bug in the model code, loss function, or optimization steps
The dataset is too small to train on
The model has achieved optimal generalization

exercises.explain: Correct! If a model can't learn a tiny subset, there is a core mathematical or logical bug in the code.

exercises.prompt: True or false: You should keep data augmentation and dropout turned ON when running the single-batch overfitting test.
exercises.explain: False. We remove all regularization (dropout, augmentation) during this test so the model can memorize the inputs easily.

exercises.sentence: If your model successfully overfits a single batch, it proves the code logic is correct, and any training issues lie in the full ___.
exercises.explain: If the code works on 4 images, the bug is likely in the full dataset's distribution, size, or quality.

exercises.prompt: What is the expected loss value when successfully overfitting a single batch of 4 images?

options.text: A value close to zero (e.g. < 0.01), representing perfect memorization
A value that spikes to infinity

exercises.explain: Correct! Perfect memorization results in near-zero loss.

exercises.prompt: You run the single-batch overfitting test with 4 images. After 100 epochs, the loss stays flat at 1.4. What should you check first?

options.text: Verify if the optimizer is actually updating weights, check if gradients are zero, and check for bugs in the custom loss function
Collect 10,000 more images to solve the learning issue
Add more layers to the CNN backbone
Assume training is complete and deploy the model

exercises.explain: Exactly! Flat loss on a tiny batch points to code bugs. Next: a systematic debugging checklist to solve CNN training issues!
